Hudson Taylor, DE Hoste, JO Fraser, A.J. Broomhall, Patrick Fung, Cambridge Seven,

(formerly **Overseas Missionary Fellowship** and before 1964 the **China Inland Mission**) is an international and [interdenominational](/wiki/Interdenominational "Interdenominational") [Evangelical](/wiki/Evangelical "Evangelical") [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") [missionary](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") society with an international centre in [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"). It was founded in [Britain](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") by [Hudson Taylor](/wiki/Hudson_Taylor "Hudson Taylor") on 25 June 1865.

was founded on theologically conservative principles by James Hudson Taylor in 1865.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1): 66 From the beginning it recruited missionaries from the working class as well as single women. The original goal of the mission that began dedicated to China has grown to include bringing the [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") to East Asia. Following the departure of all foreign workers in the early 1950s, the China Inland Mission redirected its missionaries to other parts of East Asia. The name was changed to the **Overseas Missionary Fellowship** in 1964, and then to the current name in the 1990s.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

(Redirected from [China Inland Mission](/w/index.php?title=China_Inland_Mission&redirect=no "China Inland Mission"))

International Christian missionary organization

**OMF International** (formerly **Overseas Missionary Fellowship** and before 1964 the **China Inland Mission**) is an international and [interdenominational](/wiki/Interdenominational "Interdenominational") [Evangelical](/wiki/Evangelical "Evangelical") [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") [missionary](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") society with an international centre in [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"). It was founded in [Britain](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") by [Hudson Taylor](/wiki/Hudson_Taylor "Hudson Taylor") on 25 June 1865.

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

The non-sectarian **China Inland Mission** was founded on theologically conservative principles by James Hudson Taylor in 1865.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1): 66 From the beginning it recruited missionaries from the working class as well as single women. The original goal of the mission that began dedicated to China has grown to include bringing the [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") to East Asia. Following the departure of all foreign workers in the early 1950s, the China Inland Mission redirected its missionaries to other parts of East Asia. The name was changed to the **Overseas Missionary Fellowship** in 1964, and then to the current name in the 1990s.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Missiological Distinctives of the CIM

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Missiological Distinctives of the CIM")]

> 1. Priority is given to [unreached](/wiki/Unreached_people_group "Unreached people group") inland provinces while seeking to evangelize the whole of China.
>
> 2. No solicitation of finance, or indebtedness; looking to God alone; pooling support in life of corporate faith  
> 3. Identification with Chinese by wearing Chinese dress and queue (pigtail), worshipping in Chinese houses  
> 4. Indigenization through training Chinese co-workers in self-governing, self-supporting and self-propagating principles  
> 5. Recruitment of missionaries not based on education or [ecclesiastical](/wiki/Ecclesiastical "Ecclesiastical") ordination, but spiritual qualification; deployment of single women in the interior and Christian professionals  
> 6. Interdenominational-International Membership
>
> 7. Headquarters on the field, director rule; leaders and workers serving shoulder to shoulder

— [[3]](#cite_note-3)

> "We wish to see churches and Christian Chinese presided over by pastors and officers of their own countrymen, worshipping the true God in the land of their fathers, in the costume of their fathers, in their own tongue wherein they were born, and in edifices of a thoroughly Chinese style of architecture."

— J. Hudson Taylor, [[4]](#cite_note-4)

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Origins")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/J_Hudson_Taylor_1865.jpg/120px-J_Hudson_Taylor_1865.jpg)](/wiki/File:J_Hudson_Taylor_1865.jpg)

Hudson Taylor c. 1865

[Hudson Taylor](/wiki/Hudson_Taylor "Hudson Taylor") made the first decision to found the China Inland Mission at [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton "Brighton"), [England](/wiki/England "England") during his first furlough from [China](/wiki/China "China"). Like his missionary forebear [Karl Gützlaff](/wiki/Karl_G%C3%BCtzlaff "Karl Gützlaff") and contemporary [William Chalmers Burns](/wiki/William_Chalmers_Burns "William Chalmers Burns"), Taylor was convinced that Chinese clothing should be worn when engaged in missionary work in inland China. On 3 October 1865, Taylor sent John and Anne Stevenson and [George Stott](/wiki/George_Stott_(missionary) "George Stott (missionary)") to China, where they arrived on February 6, 1866. Including the five missionaries previously sent to [Ningbo](/wiki/Ningbo "Ningbo") -[James Joseph Meadows](/wiki/James_Joseph_Meadows "James Joseph Meadows"), Jean Notman, Stephen Paul Barchet, and George and Anne Crombie, these eight were already in China when Taylor returned in 1866. On 26 May of that year, Taylor accompanied the largest group of missionaries that had ever sailed to China on the *[Lammermuir](/wiki/Lammermuir_(clipper) "Lammermuir (clipper)")*. There were 16 missionaries as well as Hudson, his wife, Maria and their 4 children that became known as the *[Lammermuir Party](/wiki/Lammermuir_Party "Lammermuir Party")*. This journey took 4 months.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Lammermuirparty.jpg/250px-Lammermuirparty.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lammermuirparty.jpg)

The Lammermuir Party sailed on 26 May 1866.

### Inland pioneering

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Inland pioneering")]

In 1872, the China Inland Mission's London council was formed. In 1875, it began to evangelise China systematically. Taylor requested 18 missionaries from God for the nine provinces which were still unreached. In 1881, he requested a further 70 missionaries, and, in 1886, 100 missionaries. In 1887 "[The Hundred missionaries](/wiki/The_Hundred_missionaries "The Hundred missionaries")" were sent to China. Taylor travelled across several continents to recruit for the China Inland Mission. By the end of the nineteenth century, the CIM was well known around the world. [Richard Lovett](/wiki/Richard_Lovett_(writer) "Richard Lovett (writer)") wrote about the practices of the missionaries in 1899. He noted that they were humble and not from the upper classes and they were having good success because they were willing to "rough it".[[5]](#cite_note-5) That year [Henrietta Soltau](/wiki/Henrietta_Soltau "Henrietta Soltau") set up a training home for women missionaries in North London. She was secretary of the ladies' council of the CIM and hundreds were trained there.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

China Inland Mission's focus on China's interior substantially expanded [rural China](/wiki/Rural_society_in_China "Rural society in China")'s exposure to Protestantism.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1): 66

### Boxer Crisis of 1900

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Boxer Crisis of 1900")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Open_Air_Preaching_WB.jpg/250px-Open_Air_Preaching_WB.jpg)](/wiki/File:Open_Air_Preaching_WB.jpg)

Missionary preaching in China using [The Wordless Book](/wiki/The_Wordless_Book "The Wordless Book")

In 1900, attacks took place across China in connection with the [Boxer Rebellion](/wiki/Boxer_Rebellion "Boxer Rebellion") which targeted Christians and foreigners. The China Inland Mission lost more members than any other agency: 58 adults and 21 children were killed. However, in 1901, when the allied nations were demanding compensation from the Chinese government, Hudson Taylor refused to accept payment for loss of property or life in order to demonstrate the meekness of Christ to the Chinese. In the same year, [Dixon Edward Hoste](/wiki/Dixon_Edward_Hoste "Dixon Edward Hoste") was appointed to the directorship of the mission.

### Growth amid war and revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Growth amid war and revolution")]

The early 1900s saw great expansion of missionary activity in China following the Boxer Rebellion, during the Revolution of 1912 and the establishment of the Chinese Republic. [William Whiting Borden](/wiki/William_Whiting_Borden "William Whiting Borden"), wealthy heir of the [Borden, Inc.](/wiki/Borden,_Inc. "Borden, Inc.") family, who graduated from [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") in 1909, left behind a comfortable life in America to respond to the call for workers with the Muslims of northwest China. He died in [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") while still in training.

A musician and an engineer named [James O. Fraser](/wiki/James_O._Fraser "James O. Fraser") was the first to bring the Gospel message to the [Lisu](/wiki/Lisu_people "Lisu people") tribes of [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan") in southwest China. This resulted in phenomenal church growth among the various tribes in the area that has endured to the 21st century.

The [Warlord period](/wiki/Warlord_Era "Warlord Era") brought widespread lawlessness to China and missionary work was often dangerous or deadly. [John and Betty Stam](/wiki/John_and_Betty_Stam "John and Betty Stam") were a young couple who were murdered in 1934 by Communist soldiers. Their biography, "The Triumph of John and Betty Stam", inspired a generation of missionaries to follow in the same steps of service despite the trials of war and persecution that raged in China in the 1930s and 1940s.

The Japanese invasion further complicated efforts as the Japanese distrusted anyone with British or American nationalities. When the Japanese invaded China in [Second Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War"), the China Inland Mission moved its headquarters upstream the [Yangzi](/wiki/Yangtze_River "Yangtze River") River to [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing"). Many missionaries were put into concentration camps, such as [Weifang](/wiki/Weifang "Weifang"), which lasted until the end of the war. The entire [Chefoo School](/wiki/Chefoo_School "Chefoo School") run by the mission at [Yantai](/wiki/Yantai "Yantai") was imprisoned at a concentration camp. As the children and teachers were marched off they sang:

> God is our refuge and strength,
>
> A very present help in trouble.
> Therefore we will not fear….
> The Lord of Hosts is with us,
>
> The God of Jacob is our refuge. (Psalm 46:1,7)

[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Despite the hardships, the number of Christians in China increased from 100,000 in early 1900s to 700,000 by 1950. The Chinese church was beginning to develop into an indigenous movement, with the assistance from key leaders such as [John Sung](/wiki/John_Sung "John Sung"), [Wang Ming-Dao](/wiki/Wang_Ming-Dao "Wang Ming-Dao"), [Watchman Nee](/wiki/Watchman_Nee "Watchman Nee") and [Andrew Gih](/wiki/Andrew_Gih "Andrew Gih").

## From CIM to OMF

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: From CIM to OMF")]

Phyllis Thompson wrote that between 1949 and 1952 in the immediate aftermath of the [Chinese Communist Revolution](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Revolution "Chinese Communist Revolution"), there was a "reluctant exodus" of all of the members of the China Inland Mission.[[7]](#cite_note-ChinaExodus-7) The leaders met at [Bournemouth](/wiki/Bournemouth "Bournemouth"), [England](/wiki/England "England") to discuss the situation and the decision was made to redeploy all of the missionaries into the rest of East Asia. Headquarters were moved to Singapore, and work commenced in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia"), [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos") and [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"). In addition to reducing some languages to written form, the Bible was translated, and basic theological education was given to neglected tribal groups. The publication and distribution of Christian literature were prioritized among both the rural tribes people and the urban working classes and students. The goal remained for every community to have a church in East Asia and thereby the Gospel would be preached "to every creature". The proclamation of the Christian message also included medical work. Three hospitals were opened in rural Thailand as well as a [leprosy](/wiki/Leprosy "Leprosy") control programme. Many of the patients were refugees. In the Philippines, community development programs were launched. [Alcoholic](/wiki/Alcoholic "Alcoholic") rehabilitation began in Japan, and rehabilitation work among prostitutes was begun in [Taipei](/wiki/Taipei "Taipei") and [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok").

In 1980, Hudson Taylor's great grandson [James Hudson Taylor III](/wiki/James_Hudson_Taylor_III "James Hudson Taylor III") was appointed General Director for the mission work. According to Taylor in 1989,

> "The fellowship has no desire to re-establish itself there (in China) in the form it used to have", but he also affirmed that "OMF is still deeply committed to the Chinese people. We can never forget that we came into existence as the China Inland Mission. Ever since our ‘reluctant exodus’ we have called the church worldwide to prayer for our brothers and sisters in China, and to share in proclaiming the gospel and nurturing millions of new believers through radio broadcasts and the provision of Bibles and Christian literature."

[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Dr. Patrick Fung, a Chinese Christian appointed in 2006, is the first Asian to lead the mission. The work continues to the present day.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## The old London headquarters building

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: The old London headquarters building")]

[![The China Inland Mission, one of two Grade 2 listed buildings on Newington Green. (October 2005).](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/China_inland_mission.jpg/250px-China_inland_mission.jpg)](/wiki/File:China_inland_mission.jpg "The China Inland Mission, one of two Grade 2 listed buildings on Newington Green. (October 2005).")

The China Inland Mission, one of two Grade 2 listed buildings on Newington Green. (October 2005).

The original headquarter was located at [Newington Green](/wiki/Newington_Green "Newington Green") in [North London](/wiki/North_London "North London"). By the late 19th century, when the CIM building was commissioned, what was once a rural village had long been subsumed into the metropolis. Newington Green had grown up around a core of [English Dissenters](/wiki/English_Dissenters "English Dissenters") and their [famous academies](/wiki/Dissenting_academies "Dissenting academies"). The CIM headquarters sits between two other [listed buildings](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building") on [the green](/wiki/Village_green "Village green"), [Newington Green Unitarian Church](/wiki/Newington_Green_Unitarian_Church "Newington Green Unitarian Church") (1708), and the oldest [brick terrace](/wiki/Terraced_house#United_Kingdom_and_Ireland "Terraced house") in London, 52-55 the Green, where the notable minister [Richard Price](/wiki/Richard_Price "Richard Price") lived.

The building was refurbished by [Haworth Tompkins](/wiki/Haworth_Tompkins "Haworth Tompkins").[[8]](#cite_note-8) Now known as Alliance House, it is run by Sanctuary Students as [accommodation](/wiki/Dormitory "Dormitory") for [City University](/wiki/City,_University_of_London "City, University of London").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Chronology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Chronology")]

### 1860s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 1860s")]

* China Inland Mission founded, 25 June 1865 in [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton "Brighton") Beach, Sussex, [England](/wiki/England "England")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/f/fa/China_Inland_Mission_%281865%29.jpg/250px-China_Inland_Mission_%281865%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:China_Inland_Mission_(1865).jpg)

Taylors and missionary candidates in 1865

* "[China's Spiritual Need and Claims](/wiki/China%27s_Spiritual_Need_and_Claims "China's Spiritual Need and Claims")" by Hudson Taylor published, October 1865 in London
* The Occasional Paper of the China Inland Mission, January 1866 is first published, in [London](/wiki/London "London")
* Lammermuir Party Sailed to China May 1866
* Lammermuir party arrived, December 1866 in 1 Xin Kai Long (New Lane), [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou"), [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang"), China
* 1866 [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang") - [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou"): (Hudson Taylor opened mission station as his headquarters).
* 1866 Zhejiang - [Fenghua](/wiki/Fenghua "Fenghua"): [George Crombie](/w/index.php?title=George_Crombie&action=edit&redlink=1 "George Crombie (page does not exist)") opened mission station.
* 1866 Zhejiang - [Shaoxing](/wiki/Shaoxing "Shaoxing"): [John Stevenson](/w/index.php?title=John_Stevenson_(missionary)&action=edit&redlink=1 "John Stevenson (missionary) (page does not exist)") & his wife Ann opened mission station.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/a7/TheNineMap.jpg/250px-TheNineMap.jpg)](/wiki/File:TheNineMap.jpg)

A map showing the nine Chinese provinces in black that were considered unreached by the [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") message in 1865

* 1867 Zhejiang - [Xiaoshan](/wiki/Xiaoshan "Xiaoshan"): Lewis & Eliza Nicol, [James Williamson](/w/index.php?title=James_Williamson_(missionary)&action=edit&redlink=1 "James Williamson (missionary) (page does not exist)") opened mission station.
* 1867 Zhejiang: John Sell dies of [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox")
* 1867 Zhejiang - [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou"): [Grace Dyer Taylor](/wiki/Grace_Dyer_Taylor "Grace Dyer Taylor"), eldest daughter of Hudson and Maria, dies of [meningitis](/wiki/Meningitis "Meningitis")
* 1867 Zhejiang - [Taizhou](/wiki/Taizhou,_Zhejiang "Taizhou, Zhejiang"): [James Joseph Meadows](/wiki/James_Joseph_Meadows "James Joseph Meadows"), Josiah Jackson opened mission station.
* 1867 [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu") - [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing"): [George Duncan](/w/index.php?title=George_Duncan_(missionary)&action=edit&redlink=1 "George Duncan (missionary) (page does not exist)") opened mission station.
* 1867 Lewis & Eliza Nicol dismissed due to conflict with Hudson Taylor
* 1868 Jiangsu - [Yangzhou](/wiki/Yangzhou "Yangzhou"): Hudson Taylor opened mission station.
* 22 August 1868: [Yangzhou riot](/wiki/Yangzhou_riot "Yangzhou riot")
* 1868 Jiangsu - [Huzhou](/wiki/Huzhou "Huzhou"): John McCarthy opened mission station.
* 1869 [Anhui](/wiki/Anhui "Anhui") - [Anqing](/wiki/Anqing "Anqing"): James Meadows, James Williamson opened mission station.
* 1869 [Jiangxi](/wiki/Jiangxi "Jiangxi") - [Jiujiang](/wiki/Jiujiang "Jiujiang"): [John Edwin Cardwell](/wiki/John_Edwin_Cardwell "John Edwin Cardwell") and wife opened mission station.

### 1870s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 1870s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Occasional_Paper_1875.jpg/250px-Occasional_Paper_1875.jpg)](/wiki/File:Occasional_Paper_1875.jpg)

Cover of the Occasional Paper of the China Inland Mission in 1875

* [Maria Jane Dyer](/wiki/Maria_Jane_Dyer "Maria Jane Dyer") "Mother of the Mission" died 23 July 1870 in [Zhenjiang](/wiki/Zhenjiang "Zhenjiang"), [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu"), China
* 1871-1875 Jiangxi - [Dagutang](/w/index.php?title=Dagutang&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dagutang (page does not exist)"): J. E. Cardwell and wife opened mission station.
* 1874 [Hubei](/wiki/Hubei "Hubei") - [Wuchang](/wiki/Wuchang,_Hubei "Wuchang, Hubei"): Hudson Taylor & Charles Judd opened mission station.
* An appeal for eighteen workers is published January 1875 in London
* [China's Millions](/w/index.php?title=China%27s_Millions&action=edit&redlink=1 "China's Millions (page does not exist)") Vol. 1, No. 1, published July 1875 in London
* 1875 [Henan](/wiki/Henan "Henan"): [Henry Taylor](/w/index.php?title=Henry_Taylor_(missionary)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Henry Taylor (missionary) (page does not exist)") is the first Protestant Christian to work in one of the 9 provinces of China so far considered unaware of the [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") message.
* 1875 [Hunan](/wiki/Hunan "Hunan"): [Charles Henry Judd](/wiki/Charles_Henry_Judd "Charles Henry Judd") and [Adam C. Dorward](/w/index.php?title=Adam_C._Dorward&action=edit&redlink=1 "Adam C. Dorward (page does not exist)") are the first Protestant Christian missionaries there and later the two travelled 1,500 miles across China from 1880-1882.
* 1876 [Shanxi](/wiki/Shanxi "Shanxi"): [Francis James](/wiki/Francis_James_(missionary) "Francis James (missionary)") and [Joshua J. Turner](/w/index.php?title=Joshua_J._Turner&action=edit&redlink=1 "Joshua J. Turner (page does not exist)") are the first Protestant Christian missionaries there and begin to help out victims of the disaster and famine.
* 1876 [Shaanxi](/wiki/Shaanxi "Shaanxi"): [Frederick W. Baller](/wiki/Frederick_W._Baller "Frederick W. Baller"), [George King](/w/index.php?title=George_King_(missionary)&action=edit&redlink=1 "George King (missionary) (page does not exist)") are the first Protestant Christian missionaries to work there.
* 1876 [Gansu](/wiki/Gansu "Gansu"): [George F. Easton](/w/index.php?title=George_F._Easton&action=edit&redlink=1 "George F. Easton (page does not exist)") and [George Parker](/w/index.php?title=George_Parker_(missionary)&action=edit&redlink=1 "George Parker (missionary) (page does not exist)") are the first Protestant Christian missionaries to work there.
* 1876 [Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan"): James Cameron is the first itinerant Protestant Christian missionary to work there. [George Nicoll](/w/index.php?title=George_Nicoll_(missionary)&action=edit&redlink=1 "George Nicoll (missionary) (page does not exist)") settles there after itineration (see also [Protestantism in Sichuan](/wiki/Protestantism_in_Sichuan "Protestantism in Sichuan")).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/8/87/Dixon_Edward_Hoste_and_fellow_China_Inland_Mission_missionaries_in_native_dress.jpg/250px-Dixon_Edward_Hoste_and_fellow_China_Inland_Mission_missionaries_in_native_dress.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dixon_Edward_Hoste_and_fellow_China_Inland_Mission_missionaries_in_native_dress.jpg)

[Dixon Edward Hoste](/wiki/Dixon_Edward_Hoste "Dixon Edward Hoste") and fellow China Inland Mission missionaries in native dress

* 1877 [Guizhou](/wiki/Guizhou "Guizhou"): Charles Henry Judd and [James F. Broumton](/w/index.php?title=James_F._Broumton&action=edit&redlink=1 "James F. Broumton (page does not exist)") are the first Protestant Christian missionaries there. Broumton later pioneered work among the [Miao](/wiki/Miao_people "Miao people") and [Yi people](/wiki/Yi_people "Yi people") minority groups.
* 1877 [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi"): [Edward Fishe](/w/index.php?title=Edward_Fishe&action=edit&redlink=1 "Edward Fishe (page does not exist)") is the first Protestant Christian missionary there. He died the same year.
* 1877 [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan"): [John McCarthy](/w/index.php?title=John_McCarthy_(missionary)&action=edit&redlink=1 "John McCarthy (missionary) (page does not exist)") traveled on foot from [Zhenjiang](/wiki/Zhenjiang "Zhenjiang") to [Hankou](/wiki/Hankou_District "Hankou District"), via Sichuan, Guizhou and Yunnan to [Bhamo](/wiki/Bhamo "Bhamo") in [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"); the trip lasted 7 months with preaching along the way. He was the first European to cross China by foot from east to west as well as the first Protestant Christian missionary to enter Yunnan Province.
* 1877 [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet"): James Cameron walked from Chongqing to [Batang](/wiki/Batang,_Sichuan "Batang, Sichuan"), the first to bring the Gospel to the Tibetan people. He then went on to Dali and Bhamo, then via [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") back to Chongqing, a journey covering 17 out of the then 18 Chinese provinces.
* 1878 Shaanxi: [Jennie Taylor](/wiki/Jane_Elizabeth_Faulding "Jane Elizabeth Faulding") is the first female Christian missionary to travel in inland China, distributing relief for those affected by the [Great North China Famine](/wiki/Great_North_China_Famine "Great North China Famine") of 1877-78.
* 1879 Shaanxi: George and Emily Snow King are the first married missionary couple to settle in [Hanzhong](/wiki/Hanzhong "Hanzhong").
* 1879 Sichuan: [M. A. Howland Nicoll](/w/index.php?title=M._A._Howland&action=edit&redlink=1 "M. A. Howland (page does not exist)") is the first female Christian missionary to live in [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing").

### 1880s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 1880s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Chinas_Millions_1885.jpg/250px-Chinas_Millions_1885.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chinas_Millions_1885.jpg)

Cover of *China's Millions* for 1885

* 1880 Shanxi: [Harold A. Schofield](/wiki/Harold_A._Schofield "Harold A. Schofield") established the first [China Inland Mission Hospital](/w/index.php?title=China_Inland_Mission_Hospital&action=edit&redlink=1 "China Inland Mission Hospital (page does not exist)") at [Taiyuan](/wiki/Taiyuan "Taiyuan").
* 1880 Gansu: [Elizabeth Wilson](/w/index.php?title=Elizabeth_Wilson_(missionary)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Elizabeth Wilson (missionary) (page does not exist)"), granddaughter of [Elizabeth Hanbury](/wiki/Elizabeth_Hanbury "Elizabeth Hanbury"), is the first female Christian missionary there.
* 1880 Guizhou: [George Clarke](/w/index.php?title=George_Clarke_(missionary)&action=edit&redlink=1 "George Clarke (missionary) (page does not exist)") and his wife Fanny settle to work there.
* 1881 Yunnan: [John Stevenson](/w/index.php?title=John_Stevenson_(missionary)&action=edit&redlink=1 "John Stevenson (missionary) (page does not exist)") and [Henry Soltau](/w/index.php?title=Henry_Soltau&action=edit&redlink=1 "Henry Soltau (page does not exist)") travelled 1,900 miles in 86 days on foot from Bhamo, [Kunming](/wiki/Kunming "Kunming"), Chongqing, [Wuchang](/wiki/Wuchang,_Hubei "Wuchang, Hubei") to Shanghai, setting a record from west to east.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/8/8b/AmericanCIM.jpg/250px-AmericanCIM.jpg)](/wiki/File:AmericanCIM.jpg)

First Party of Americans to join the CIM in 1888

* 1881 Yunnan: George and Fanny Clarke settle to work in [Dali](/wiki/Dali,_Yunnan "Dali, Yunnan").
* 1881 [Shandong](/wiki/Shandong "Shandong"): [Chefoo School](/wiki/Chefoo_School "Chefoo School") begun at [Yantai](/wiki/Yantai "Yantai") (originally "The Protestant Collegiate School")
* [Cambridge Seven](/wiki/Cambridge_Seven "Cambridge Seven") arrived 3 March 1885 in China.
* [The Hundred missionaries](/wiki/The_Hundred_missionaries "The Hundred missionaries") sent out in one year 1887.
* Benjamin Broomhall launches *[National Righteousness](/w/index.php?title=National_Righteousness&action=edit&redlink=1 "National Righteousness (page does not exist)")*, an [Anti-Opium Campaign](/w/index.php?title=Anti-Opium_Campaign&action=edit&redlink=1 "Anti-Opium Campaign (page does not exist)") periodical in 1888.
* First party of [American](/wiki/United_States "United States") missionaries arrived 30 October 1888 in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), China.
* 1889: North America Home Council formed.

### 1890s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 1890s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/3/3e/China_Inland_Mission_headquarters%2C_Shanghai_%28late_19th_century%29.jpg/250px-China_Inland_Mission_headquarters%2C_Shanghai_%28late_19th_century%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:China_Inland_Mission_headquarters,_Shanghai_(late_19th_century).jpg)

The China Inland Mission Headquarters in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"). Late 1800s.

* Shanghai Headquarters at Wusong Road 1890.
* 1890 [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") Home Council for CIM formed.
* First party of Australian missionaries arrived in 1890.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/e/e0/AustralianCIM.jpg/250px-AustralianCIM.jpg)](/wiki/File:AustralianCIM.jpg)

First Party of Australians to join the CIM in 1890

* [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") Home Council formed.
* [Newington Green](/wiki/Newington_Green "Newington Green") office headquarters in London 1895.

### 1900s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 1900s")]

* [Boxer Rebellion](/wiki/Boxer_Rebellion "Boxer Rebellion") of 1900 claims 58 missionaries and 21 children killed from the China Inland Mission.
* In 1901 Hudson Taylor refused to accept compensation payment from the Chinese government for loss of property or life, to show the 'meekness and gentleness of Christ'
* 1901, A council was set up, headquartered in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), to supervise the mission's work in the United States
* [Dixon Edward Hoste](/wiki/Dixon_Edward_Hoste "Dixon Edward Hoste") appointed acting General Director in 1901

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/CIM1902.jpg/250px-CIM1902.jpg)](/wiki/File:CIM1902.jpg)

CIM missions as of 1902

* James Hudson Taylor resigned as Director of the China Inland Mission November 1902
* 1904 Xinjiang: [George Hunter](/wiki/George_Hunter_(missionary) "George Hunter (missionary)") opens a mission station
* James Hudson Taylor died 3 June 1905 in [Changsha](/wiki/Changsha "Changsha"), [Hunan](/wiki/Hunan "Hunan"), China
* Empress Dowager dies in 1908
* China Inland Mission sent relief team to flood and famine in [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu"), [Anhui](/wiki/Anhui "Anhui") and [Henan](/wiki/Henan "Henan")

### 1910s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 1910s")]

* [J. O. Fraser](/wiki/J._O._Fraser "J. O. Fraser") arrived in China in 1910
* 60,000 Christians in West [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan"), China tribal region
* 1911 [Benjamin Broomhall](/wiki/Benjamin_Broomhall "Benjamin Broomhall") died after his [Anti-Opium Campaign](/w/index.php?title=Anti-Opium_Campaign&action=edit&redlink=1 "Anti-Opium Campaign (page does not exist)") succeeds
* [Chinese Republic](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%9349) "Republic of China (1912–49)") established in 1912
* In 1912 membership in the China Inland Mission exceeds 1,000, then the largest mission agency working in China
* 1915 1,063 workers were working at 227 stations.

### 1920s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 1920s")]

* 1925 Gustav Burklin arrives in China
* 1927 The [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War") forced a temporary evacuation of nearly all of the missionaries
* 1929-1932 The China Inland Mission is This was part of 'The Forward Movement' as the Missionaries moved back to their stations.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The 'Call for Two Hundred New Workers' was issued in March 1929 and shared in China's Millions in May 1929[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### 1930s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: 1930s")]

* Headquarters in Shanghai move to Sinza Road in 1930
* By 31 Dec 1931 the last of the Two Hundred New Workers had sailed.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* 1934 1,368 missionaries were serving at 364 stations. The mission staff also included hundreds of Chinese pastors, teachers, colporteurs, chapel keepers and Bible women.
* [John and Betty Stam](/wiki/John_and_Betty_Stam "John and Betty Stam") executed in South [Anhui](/wiki/Anhui "Anhui") in 1934
* [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") forced many of the missionaries further inland – or they were captured by the Japanese and detained until the end of the war
* By 1939, 200,000 Chinese and Minority people had been baptised by CIM workers

### 1940s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: 1940s")]

* 1942, 1,263 missionaries. The headquarters was evacuated out of Shanghai to escape the Japanese army. An emergency headquarters was set up in [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing"), the same city where the Chinese government had relocated.
* November 1942 China Inland Mission School at Chefoo ([Yantai](/wiki/Yantai "Yantai")) is closed and all students and staff imprisoned.
* 1943, [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") Home Council formed
* August 1945 China Inland Mission School at Chefoo ([Yantai](/wiki/Yantai "Yantai")) is liberated by American paratroopers
* 1945, The staff moved back to [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai")
* 1 October 1949 [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") proclaims [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing")
* Werner Bürklin. son of Gustav and Lina Bürklin was born in Wuhu, Province Anhui

### 1950s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: 1950s")]

* After "[The Christian Manifesto](/wiki/The_Christian_Manifesto "The Christian Manifesto")", the China Inland Mission began to withdraw its missionaries ending in 1953
* 1950, 1,104 missionaries, of whom 757 were in China. CIM home council started in Switzerland
* 1951 [Three-Self Patriotic Movement](/wiki/Three-Self_Patriotic_Movement "Three-Self Patriotic Movement") launched allowing government control of Christian assembly
* In November 1951, a new headquarters was set up in Singapore, and the organisations name was changed to The China Inland Mission Overseas Missionary Fellowship
* 1951, A temporary headquarters was set up in Hong Kong, mainly to oversee the withdrawal of the missionaries.
* 14 October 1954, The mission was reorganised at a meeting of the mission's overseas council. The council reaffirmed the need for the mission, but changed its structure so that non-Western Christians could become full members and set up home councils in their own countries. The main emphasis of the OMF was to continue to be evangelism, but support would also be given to a literature programme, medical services, radio and TV outreach, student work and linguistic work.
* Re-deployment of all missionaries to East Asia

### 1960s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: 1960s")]

* China Inland Mission renamed [Overseas Missionary Fellowship](/wiki/Overseas_Missionary_Fellowship "Overseas Missionary Fellowship") in 1964
* [Chinese Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Chinese_Cultural_Revolution "Chinese Cultural Revolution") 1966–1972: all Christians silenced including the [Three-Self Patriotic Movement](/wiki/Three-Self_Patriotic_Movement "Three-Self Patriotic Movement")
* Medical work begun in rural Thailand
* 1965 [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") and [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") Home Councils formed
* 1966 [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") Home Councils formed
* 1967 [German](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") and [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") Home Councils formed
* 1969 The council for North America was dissolved and the U.S. and Canadian councils became completely autonomous.

### 1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: 1970s")]

* 1975 Merger with [Borneo Evangelical Mission](/wiki/Borneo_Evangelical_Mission "Borneo Evangelical Mission") (BEM)

### 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: 1980s")]

* 1980-1990 James Hudson Taylor III served as seventh General Director.
* Chinese Church reaches 21.5 million baptised members, over 52 million including Christian families and adherents
* Werner Bürklin, born in China, returns to China in 1980 to start a teaching ministry, first in Nanchang, Jiangxi and then in many cities across China. He founded China Partner, a mission organisation to facilitate this. Later his son Erik alongside his daughter Linda continued this ministry.

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: 1990s")]

* Overseas Missionary Fellowship renamed OMF International
* 1993 First OMF workers enter Mongolia
* 1994 AIDS ward opens at Manorom Hospital, Thailand
* 1995 OMF establishes web presence
* 1999 OMF workers involved in relief operation after [Taiwan earthquake](/wiki/1999_Jiji_earthquake "1999 Jiji earthquake")

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: 2000s")]

* 2004 OMF involved in relief after [Asian tsunami](/wiki/2004_Indian_Ocean_earthquake_and_tsunami "2004 Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami")
* 2006 Dr Patrick Fung is appointed as General Director, the first Asian to hold this position in OMF.
* 2008 OMF involved in relief work, including counselling, after the [2008 Sichuan earthquake](/wiki/2008_Sichuan_earthquake "2008 Sichuan earthquake").

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: 2010s")]

* 2011 OMF involved in relief, counselling and visiting temporary housing following [2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami](/wiki/2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami "2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami")
* 2014 OMF reopens its OMF Southern Africa centre, based in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa")
* 2015 OMF Celebrates 150 years: 1865-2015.
* 2016 OMF joins the [Faith2Share](http://www.faith2share.net) network of mission agencies.
* OMF International has 1400 workers from over 40 nations, serving globally.

## Archives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=OMF_International&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Archives")]

The papers of the China Inland Mission are held by [SOAS Archives](http://www.soas.ac.uk/library/archives/), the [Billy Graham Center](/wiki/Billy_Graham_Center "Billy Graham Center") for Evangelism at [Wheaton College](/wiki/Wheaton_College_(Illinois) "Wheaton College (Illinois)") [[1]](http://www2.wheaton.edu/bgc/archives/GUIDES/215.htm), the OMF International Center in Singapore [[2]](https://omf.org/about-omf/mission-research/archives/), the Hong Kong Baptist University, and several regional offices.
